learning_objectives: Define cloud computing characteristics as specified by NIST and explain each one
Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service models with concrete provider examples
Evaluate when cloud deployment is appropriate versus on-premises infrastructure
readings: Armbrust et al., 'A View of Cloud Computing' (Communications of the ACM, 2010)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud computing is the most significant transformation in IT infrastructure since the invention of the internet. It fundamentally changed how organizations provision, manage, and pay for computing resources, shifting from a capital expenditure model of buying and maintaining physical servers to an operational expenditure model of renting compute capacity by the second. Today, the three major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud Platform) collectively operate millions of servers across dozens of data center regions worldwide, offering hundreds of managed services that abstract away everything from hardware maintenance to operating system patching to database administration. This course teaches you to architect, deploy, and operate applications on cloud infrastructure with a focus on AWS as the primary platform, while covering the concepts that transfer across all major providers. We begin by establishing the foundational vocabulary and mental models that underpin every cloud architecture decision.
key_concepts: NIST five essential characteristics: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service
Service models: IaaS provides virtual machines (EC2), PaaS provides managed runtimes (Elastic Beanstalk, App Engine), SaaS provides complete applications (Gmail, Salesforce)
Deployment models: public cloud (shared infrastructure), private cloud (dedicated infrastructure), hybrid cloud (connected public and private)
Shared responsibility model: provider secures the infrastructure (physical, network, hypervisor), customer secures their configuration, data, and access controls
Cloud economics: pay-per-use eliminates upfront capital expenditure, enables experimentation, but requires cost monitoring to avoid surprise bills
Total cost of ownership: compare cloud costs against on-premises including hardware, power, cooling, staffing, and opportunity cost of provisioning delays
Multi-cloud strategy: using multiple providers to avoid vendor lock-in, leverage best-of-breed services, or meet geographic requirements
Cloud-native vs lift-and-shift: redesigning applications for the cloud versus migrating existing applications unchanged
Well-Architected Framework pillars: operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, sustainability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Create an AWS account, navigate the management console, launch an EC2 instance via the console and then via the AWS CLI, SSH into it, deploy a simple web application, and examine the billing dashboard to understand the cost of running the instance
setup_requirements: AWS account with Free Tier enabled
AWS CLI v2 installed and configured with access keys
SSH key pair generated for EC2 access
Terminal with SSH client

review.value: AWS secures the cloud (physical data centers, hypervisors, managed service infrastructure); you secure what you put in the cloud (EC2 configurations, S3 bucket policies, IAM roles, application code). The majority of cloud security breaches stem from customer misconfiguration, not provider failure

Scalability is the ability to handle growing demand by adding resources. Elasticity is the ability to automatically scale resources both up when demand increases and down when it decreases. Elasticity is what makes cloud cost-effective: you pay for capacity only when you need it

Cloud excels for variable workloads, experimentation, global distribution, and when time-to-market matters. On-premises may be better for steady-state workloads with predictable demand, strict data sovereignty requirements, or when you have existing data center investment

AWS Free Tier includes 750 hours/month of t2.micro EC2, 5GB S3, and limited usage of dozens of services for 12 months. Exceeding these limits incurs charges immediately. Always set billing alarms and review the billing dashboard weekly during this course

learning_objectives: Explain how hypervisor-based virtualization works at the hardware and OS level
Compare Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors with their security and performance characteristics
Configure and manage virtual machines on AWS EC2 with different instance types
readings: Tanenbaum, Modern Operating Systems, Ch. 7 - Virtualization
VMware, 'Understanding Full Virtualization, Paravirtualization, and Hardware Assist'

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtualization is the foundational technology that makes cloud computing possible. By abstracting physical hardware into multiple isolated virtual machines, virtualization enables cloud providers to share a single physical server among many customers while maintaining the illusion that each customer has a dedicated machine. This multi-tenancy is what drives the economics of cloud computing: the provider achieves high utilization of expensive hardware, and the customer pays only for the resources they consume. Understanding virtualization is essential for cloud architects because it explains the performance characteristics, security boundaries, and limitations of cloud instances. This session covers the evolution of virtualization from software-based emulation through paravirtualization to hardware-assisted virtualization, explores how modern hypervisors like KVM and Xen power AWS EC2, and teaches you to select the right instance type for your workload based on an understanding of the underlying virtual hardware allocation.
key_concepts: Hypervisor definition: software that creates and manages virtual machines by abstracting physical hardware
Type 1 hypervisors (bare-metal): run directly on hardware (KVM, Xen, ESXi, Hyper-V), used by cloud providers for performance and security
Type 2 hypervisors (hosted): run on top of a host OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation), used for development and testing
Hardware-assisted virtualization: Intel VT-x and AMD-V provide CPU support for efficient VM execution
Virtual machine components: virtual CPU, virtual memory, virtual disk, virtual network interface
EC2 instance families: general purpose (M/T), compute-optimized (C), memory-optimized (R), storage-optimized (I/D), GPU (P/G)
Instance sizing: vCPU count, memory, network bandwidth, and storage throughput for each instance type
Nitro System: AWS's custom hypervisor and hardware offload cards that improve performance and security
Live migration: moving a running VM between physical hosts without downtime for hardware maintenance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Launch three different EC2 instance types (t3.micro, c5.large, r5.large), run CPU and memory benchmarks on each, compare performance characteristics, examine the instance metadata service to see the virtual hardware configuration, and analyze the cost per benchmark unit
setup_requirements: AWS account with EC2 permissions
SSH key pair for instance access
Benchmarking tools: sysbench for CPU, stress-ng for memory

review.value: Type 1 runs directly on hardware with no host OS, providing better performance and stronger isolation (used in production cloud). Type 2 runs as an application on a host OS, adding overhead but enabling easy desktop use. Cloud computing exclusively uses Type 1 for security and performance

Match the instance family to your workload: T-series for burstable general-purpose loads (web servers), C-series for sustained CPU (batch processing), R-series for memory-intensive (in-memory databases), P/G-series for GPU (ML training). Over-provisioning wastes money; under-provisioning causes performance problems

T-series instances earn CPU credits when idle and spend them during bursts. If credits are exhausted, performance is throttled to a baseline. Good for variable workloads like development servers; dangerous for sustained high-CPU workloads where throttling causes user-visible slowdowns

Multiple customers' VMs run on the same physical hardware. The hypervisor provides isolation, but vulnerabilities like Spectre and Meltdown showed side-channel leakage is possible. For highly sensitive workloads, use dedicated instances or dedicated hosts that guarantee no hardware sharing

learning_objectives: Explain how containers use Linux namespaces and cgroups for isolation without a hypervisor
Write Dockerfiles that follow best practices for layer caching, security, and image size
Build, tag, push, and manage container images using Docker and container registries
readings: Linux Journal, 'Understanding Linux Namespaces and Cgroups'

lecture_notes.introduction: Containers have become the standard deployment unit for cloud applications, fundamentally changing how software is packaged, distributed, and run. Unlike virtual machines that include a full operating system, containers share the host OS kernel and isolate processes using Linux kernel features: namespaces for isolation and cgroups for resource limits. This makes containers dramatically lighter than VMs, starting in seconds rather than minutes and consuming megabytes rather than gigabytes. Docker popularized containers by providing a simple developer experience: define your application's environment in a Dockerfile, build it into a portable image, and run it identically on any machine with Docker installed. This portability eliminates the 'works on my machine' problem and enables consistent environments from development through production. This session teaches you to containerize applications effectively, understanding not just the Docker commands but the underlying Linux mechanisms that make containers work, the image layering system that enables efficient builds, and the security considerations that differ from traditional VM isolation.
key_concepts: Container vs VM: containers share the host kernel (MB-sized, seconds to start); VMs include a full OS (GB-sized, minutes to start)
Linux namespaces: PID, network, mount, UTS, IPC, and user namespaces provide process-level isolation
Cgroups: kernel feature that limits and monitors CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network usage per container
Docker architecture: Docker daemon, Docker client, images, containers, registries, and the containerd runtime
Dockerfile instructions: FROM, RUN, COPY, ADD, ENV, EXPOSE, CMD, ENTRYPOINT, and their execution order
Image layers: each Dockerfile instruction creates a read-only layer; layers are cached and shared between images
Multi-stage builds: using separate build and runtime stages to minimize final image size (build tools not shipped)
Container registries: Docker Hub, AWS ECR, Google Artifact Registry for storing and distributing images
Docker Compose: defining multi-container applications with networking, volumes, and environment configuration
Container security: running as non-root, scanning for vulnerabilities, using minimal base images (Alpine, distroless)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Containerize a Node.js web application: write a Dockerfile with multi-stage build (npm install in build stage, copy only production artifacts to runtime stage), reduce image size from 900MB to 80MB, push to Amazon ECR, and run with Docker Compose alongside a PostgreSQL container
setup_requirements: Docker Desktop installed and running
AWS CLI configured with ECR permissions
Sample Node.js application to containerize
Docker Compose v2 available

review.value: Docker caches each layer and reuses it if the instruction and its context have not changed. COPY requirements.txt before COPY . so that dependency installation is cached when only application code changes. This reduces build times from minutes to seconds for code-only changes

Containers share the host kernel, so a kernel vulnerability can affect all containers. VMs have stronger isolation through the hypervisor. Run containers as non-root, use read-only filesystems where possible, scan images for known vulnerabilities, and use security profiles like seccomp and AppArmor

Build tools, compilers, and development dependencies are needed to build the application but not to run it. Multi-stage builds use a full SDK image for building and copy only the compiled output to a minimal runtime image. This reduces attack surface and image size dramatically

Docker Compose defines all services (app, database, cache, message queue) in a single YAML file. One command starts the entire stack with proper networking. This ensures every developer runs the same environment regardless of their host OS or installed software

learning_objectives: Describe Kubernetes architecture including control plane components and worker nodes
Deploy applications to Kubernetes using Deployments, Services, and ConfigMaps
Configure horizontal pod autoscaling based on CPU utilization and custom metrics
readings: Burns et al., 'Kubernetes: Up and Running', Ch. 1-6
The Kubernetes Book by Nigel Poulton, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Running a single container on a single machine is simple. Running hundreds of containers across dozens of machines while handling failures, scaling, networking, and updates is the hard problem that Kubernetes solves. Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform originally developed by Google based on their internal Borg system. It automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications across clusters of machines. Kubernetes has become the de facto standard for container orchestration, supported by every major cloud provider through managed services (EKS on AWS, GKE on Google Cloud, AKS on Azure). This session teaches the core Kubernetes concepts and objects you need to deploy and manage production applications: pods as the atomic deployment unit, deployments for managing replicas and updates, services for stable network endpoints, and autoscaling for responding to load changes. Understanding these primitives is essential for modern cloud-native architecture.
key_concepts: Kubernetes architecture: control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager) and worker nodes (kubelet, container runtime)
Pod: smallest deployable unit containing one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing network and storage
Deployment: declares desired state (image, replicas, update strategy); controller reconciles actual state to match
Service: stable network endpoint (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer) that routes traffic to pods by label selector
ConfigMap and Secret: externalize configuration and sensitive data from container images
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA): automatically adjusts replica count based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics
Rolling updates: deploying new versions by incrementally replacing pods, with rollback on failure
Namespaces: logical isolation for multi-tenant clusters or environment separation (dev, staging, prod)
kubectl: command-line tool for interacting with the Kubernetes API (apply, get, describe, logs, exec)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy a web application to a local Kubernetes cluster (minikube or kind): create a Deployment with 3 replicas, expose it with a LoadBalancer Service, configure environment variables via ConfigMap, set up HPA to scale between 2 and 10 pods based on CPU, and perform a rolling update to a new version with automatic rollback
setup_requirements: minikube or kind installed for local Kubernetes
kubectl configured and connected to the cluster
Container image pushed to a registry accessible from the cluster
metrics-server deployed for autoscaling

review.value: kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml is declarative: you define the desired state and Kubernetes converges to it. kubectl run is imperative: you tell Kubernetes what to do step by step. Always use declarative manifests in production so your cluster state is version-controlled and reproducible

A pod can contain multiple containers that share the same network namespace (localhost communication) and storage volumes. Use multi-container pods for sidecar patterns (logging agent, proxy, init container) but run one application container per pod for independent scaling

Kubernetes Services provide a stable DNS name and IP address for a set of pods. When pods are created or destroyed (scaling, updates, failures), the Service automatically updates its endpoints. Application code connects to the Service name, never to pod IPs directly

Set maxUnavailable and maxSurge to control update speed vs availability. Use readiness probes so Kubernetes only sends traffic to pods that are ready. Set a progressDeadline to automatically roll back if the new version fails to become ready within the timeout

learning_objectives: Map equivalent services across AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud Platform
Evaluate provider strengths for different workload types and organizational contexts
Navigate the console, CLI, and SDK for each major provider
readings: Cloud Provider Service Comparison Matrix (course handout)
Google Cloud Documentation: Choosing the Right Compute Option

lecture_notes.introduction: While AWS dominates cloud market share at approximately 32%, Azure (23%) and Google Cloud Platform (11%) offer compelling alternatives with different strengths, pricing models, and service portfolios. Enterprise organizations increasingly adopt multi-cloud strategies, using different providers for different workloads based on each provider's strengths. AWS has the broadest service catalog and largest ecosystem. Azure integrates deeply with Microsoft enterprise tools (Active Directory, Office 365, .NET). Google Cloud leads in data analytics, machine learning, and Kubernetes (as the creator of the technology). Understanding the service mappings and trade-offs across providers is essential for cloud architects who must recommend the right platform for each use case. This session provides a comprehensive comparison of the three major providers, maps equivalent services across platforms, examines pricing and support differences, and establishes the multi-cloud knowledge base you need for the remainder of this course and your career.
key_concepts: Market positioning: AWS as the broadest, Azure as the enterprise-integrated, GCP as the developer and data-focused
Compute service mapping: EC2 / Azure Virtual Machines / Compute Engine for IaaS VMs
Managed Kubernetes: EKS (AWS) / AKS (Azure) / GKE (Google, most mature and opinionated)
Serverless mapping: Lambda / Azure Functions / Cloud Functions with different cold start and runtime characteristics
Storage mapping: S3 / Azure Blob Storage / Cloud Storage for object storage with similar durability guarantees
Database mapping: RDS / Azure SQL Database / Cloud SQL for managed relational; DynamoDB / Cosmos DB / Firestore for NoSQL
Pricing models: on-demand, reserved/committed use, spot/preemptible instances across all three providers
Global infrastructure: AWS has 33 regions, Azure has 60+ regions, GCP has 40 regions with different geographic coverage
Identity management: AWS IAM / Azure Active Directory / Google Cloud IAM with different authentication integration points
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy the same containerized application to all three providers: create a small Kubernetes cluster on EKS, AKS, and GKE, deploy the identical container image, expose it with a load balancer, compare the setup complexity, deployment time, and resulting monthly cost estimate for each
setup_requirements: Accounts on AWS, Azure, and GCP with free tier credits
CLI tools: aws, az, gcloud installed and configured
kubectl configured for all three clusters
Container image in a publicly accessible registry

review.value: Choose based on: existing organizational investment (Microsoft shops lean Azure), specific service needs (GCP for BigQuery analytics, AWS for broadest catalog), pricing (committed use discounts vary), compliance requirements (government regions), and team expertise (retraining cost)

Google created Kubernetes and GKE is the most mature managed Kubernetes service: automatic control plane upgrades, integrated monitoring, Autopilot mode for fully managed nodes, and the fastest feature adoption. If Kubernetes is central to your architecture, GKE deserves strong consideration

VMs are highly portable (same OS everywhere). Containers add slight lock-in (registry format differences). Managed services add significant lock-in (DynamoDB APIs do not exist on GCP). Evaluate the portability cost of each service choice against the productivity benefit of using it

Multi-cloud avoids vendor lock-in but multiplies operational complexity: different IAM models, different networking, different monitoring tools, different billing. Most organizations benefit more from deep expertise in one provider than shallow knowledge of three

learning_objectives: Select the appropriate compute service (EC2, ECS, Fargate, Lambda) for different workload patterns
Configure auto-scaling groups with launch templates, scaling policies, and health checks
Implement load balancing with Application Load Balancer for HTTP traffic distribution
readings: AWS Documentation: Choosing Between EC2, ECS, Fargate, and Lambda

lecture_notes.introduction: Compute is the most fundamental cloud resource, and AWS offers a spectrum of compute services that trade management overhead for operational control. At one end, EC2 gives you full virtual machines where you manage everything from the OS up. ECS and EKS manage container orchestration while you manage the container images. Fargate removes server management entirely for containers. Lambda eliminates even container management, running individual functions in response to events. Choosing the right compute service is one of the most impactful architectural decisions because it determines your operational burden, scaling characteristics, cost structure, and deployment model for the lifetime of the application. This session covers the full AWS compute spectrum, focusing on how to evaluate workload requirements against service capabilities, how to configure auto-scaling for cost-efficient elastic capacity, and how to distribute traffic across compute resources using Application Load Balancers for high availability and zero-downtime deployments.
key_concepts: EC2 compute spectrum: on-demand for flexibility, reserved instances for predictable workloads, spot instances for fault-tolerant batch jobs (60-90% savings)
Launch templates: defining instance configuration (AMI, instance type, security groups, user data) for reproducible deployments
Auto Scaling Groups: maintaining desired capacity with min/max bounds, replacing unhealthy instances automatically
Scaling policies: target tracking (maintain 70% CPU), step scaling (add 2 instances when CPU > 80%), scheduled scaling (scale up at 9am)
Application Load Balancer: HTTP/HTTPS traffic distribution with path-based routing, host-based routing, and health checks
ECS with Fargate: serverless container execution without managing EC2 instances, paying only for vCPU and memory used
Spot instances: bidding on unused EC2 capacity at deep discounts with 2-minute interruption warning
Placement groups: cluster (low latency between instances), spread (high availability across hardware), partition (large distributed systems)
Instance metadata service (IMDS): runtime access to instance identity, IAM role credentials, and user data from within the instance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy a web application on EC2 with an Auto Scaling Group and Application Load Balancer: create a launch template, configure target tracking scaling at 70% CPU, set up ALB with health checks, then generate load to observe auto-scaling add instances and the ALB distribute traffic across them
setup_requirements: AWS account with EC2, ELB, and Auto Scaling permissions
Web application AMI or launch template with user data script
Load testing tool (Apache Bench or hey)
AWS CLI for monitoring scaling activity

review.value: Long-running stateful: EC2. Containerized microservices: ECS/EKS with Fargate. Short-lived event-driven: Lambda. Batch processing with cost sensitivity: EC2 Spot or Fargate Spot. The decision is primarily about management overhead vs control: more management means lower cost but higher operational burden

After a scaling action, the cooldown period prevents additional scaling for a configured duration. Without cooldown, oscillation occurs: scale up triggers high spend, load drops, scale down triggers, load spikes again. Default is 300 seconds; tune based on your application's startup time

Health checks determine when instances receive traffic. Configure the health check path to verify real application health (not just HTTP 200 from a load balancer status page). Set appropriate intervals and thresholds so unhealthy instances are removed quickly without false positives during deployment

Spot instances can be interrupted with 2-minute warning. Design for interruption: use multiple instance types and availability zones, persist work to durable storage, use Spot Fleet to maintain capacity across instance types. Never run stateful workloads on Spot without external state persistence

learning_objectives: Select the appropriate storage service for different data access patterns and durability requirements
Configure S3 buckets with lifecycle policies, versioning, and cross-region replication
Deploy managed databases (RDS, DynamoDB) with backup, replication, and failover strategies

lecture_notes.introduction: Data is the most valuable asset in any application, and choosing the wrong storage service leads to poor performance, data loss risk, and runaway costs. AWS offers dozens of storage and database services, each optimized for specific access patterns, durability requirements, and cost profiles. Object storage (S3) handles unstructured data with unlimited scale. Block storage (EBS) provides low-latency disk for EC2 instances. File storage (EFS) offers shared filesystems for multiple instances. Relational databases (RDS, Aurora) handle structured data with SQL queries and ACID transactions. NoSQL databases (DynamoDB) provide single-digit millisecond latency for key-value and document access patterns. Caching layers (ElastiCache) reduce database load for read-heavy workloads. This session teaches you to evaluate your data access patterns and match them to the right service, configure each service for production use with appropriate backup and replication, and understand the cost implications of different storage tiers and access patterns.
key_concepts: S3: object storage with 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability, unlimited capacity, storage classes from Standard to Glacier Deep Archive
S3 lifecycle policies: automatically transition objects between storage classes based on age (Standard -> IA -> Glacier)
S3 versioning: maintaining all versions of an object for recovery from accidental deletion or overwrite
EBS: block storage volumes attached to EC2, types include gp3 (general SSD), io2 (high IOPS SSD), st1 (throughput HDD)
RDS: managed relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Aurora) with automated backups, patching, and Multi-AZ failover
Aurora: AWS-designed relational engine with 5x MySQL performance, 6-way replication, and automatic storage scaling
DynamoDB: serverless NoSQL with single-digit millisecond latency, on-demand or provisioned capacity, global tables for multi-region
ElastiCache: managed Redis or Memcached for caching database queries, session data, and API responses
Storage cost optimization: right-sizing EBS volumes, S3 Intelligent-Tiering for unknown access patterns, DynamoDB on-demand for unpredictable workloads
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy a production-like storage architecture: create an S3 bucket with versioning and lifecycle policies transitioning to Glacier after 90 days, deploy a PostgreSQL RDS instance with Multi-AZ failover and automated daily backups, set up DynamoDB for session storage with on-demand capacity, and configure ElastiCache Redis for query caching
setup_requirements: AWS account with S3, RDS, DynamoDB, and ElastiCache permissions
Database client (psql or pgAdmin) for RDS
AWS CLI for DynamoDB operations
Sample data for testing different storage patterns

review.value: S3 provides 99.999999999% durability (losing one object in 10 million is expected once every 10,000 years) by replicating across at least 3 AZs. Availability is 99.99% for Standard class. Durability means your data will not be lost; availability means you can access it at any given time

Multi-AZ provides high availability: a synchronous standby in another AZ takes over automatically on primary failure (failover in 60-120 seconds). Read replicas provide read scalability: asynchronous copies serve read queries. Multi-AZ is for availability; read replicas are for performance. Use both in production

DynamoDB distributes data across partitions by hashing the partition key. A hot partition (one key receiving disproportionate traffic) causes throttling even if total table capacity is sufficient. Design partition keys for even distribution: use composite keys or add random suffixes for write-heavy patterns

Cache data that is read frequently and changes infrequently. Set appropriate TTL based on staleness tolerance. Use cache-aside pattern: check cache first, on miss fetch from database and populate cache. Invalidate cache on write operations to prevent stale reads

learning_objectives: Design VPC architectures with public and private subnets across multiple availability zones
Configure security groups, network ACLs, and route tables for defense-in-depth network security
Implement DNS management with Route 53 and content delivery with CloudFront
readings: AWS VPC Documentation: VPC and Subnet Basics

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud networking is the invisible infrastructure that connects all your cloud resources and determines who can access what. A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) is your isolated network within the AWS cloud, and how you design it determines the security posture, availability characteristics, and connectivity options of your entire architecture. Production VPCs use multiple availability zones for fault tolerance, separate public and private subnets to isolate internet-facing resources from backend systems, and layer security controls at the network level using security groups and NACLs. Beyond the VPC, DNS routing with Route 53 directs users to your application, CloudFront CDN caches content at edge locations for low-latency global delivery, and VPN or Direct Connect provides secure connectivity to on-premises networks. This session teaches you to design VPC architectures that follow AWS best practices, configure network security controls, and set up DNS and CDN for production applications.
key_concepts: VPC: isolated virtual network with configurable IP address range (CIDR block), spanning multiple availability zones
Subnets: subdivisions of a VPC CIDR, each in a single AZ; public subnets have internet gateway routes, private subnets do not
Internet Gateway: allows instances in public subnets to communicate with the internet
NAT Gateway: allows instances in private subnets to make outbound internet requests without being directly reachable from the internet
Security Groups: stateful firewalls attached to instances allowing traffic by protocol, port, and source (other security groups or CIDR blocks)
Network ACLs: stateless firewalls at the subnet level for additional defense-in-depth (allow and deny rules processed in order)
Route Tables: determine where network traffic is directed, associated with subnets
Route 53: managed DNS service with routing policies (simple, weighted, latency-based, failover, geolocation)
CloudFront: CDN with 400+ edge locations for caching static content and accelerating dynamic content delivery globally
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design and deploy a production VPC: create a VPC with 2 public and 2 private subnets across 2 AZs, configure internet gateway for public subnets and NAT gateway for private subnets, set up security groups that allow only HTTPS inbound to the ALB and only ALB-to-app traffic to private instances, deploy the web application with Route 53 DNS and CloudFront CDN
setup_requirements: AWS account with VPC, EC2, Route 53, and CloudFront permissions
Domain name registered in Route 53 or external DNS
Network diagram tool for architecture documentation
AWS CLI for VPC configuration

review.value: Public subnets: only for resources that must be directly internet-accessible (load balancers, bastion hosts). Private subnets: everything else (application servers, databases, caches). Never place a database in a public subnet. Use NAT Gateway for private instances that need outbound internet access (package updates, API calls)

Security groups are stateful: if you allow inbound traffic, the response is automatically allowed. Reference other security groups as sources instead of CIDR blocks: allow inbound to the app security group only from the ALB security group. This ties security to identity rather than IP addresses

Deploy subnets in at least 2 AZs for fault tolerance. Each AZ is a physically separate data center. If one AZ fails (rare but possible), your application continues running in the other AZ. ALB automatically routes traffic away from the failed AZ

CloudFront caches content at edge locations based on TTL. For frequently updated content, use versioned URLs (app.v2.js) instead of cache invalidation. Invalidation requests propagate to all 400+ edges and take minutes. Versioned URLs take effect immediately when the URL changes

learning_objectives: Build serverless applications with AWS Lambda triggered by API Gateway, S3, and SQS events
Design event-driven architectures that decouple services through asynchronous message passing
Evaluate the trade-offs of serverless versus container-based deployment for different workloads
readings: AWS Well-Architected Framework: Serverless Application Lens

lecture_notes.introduction: Serverless computing represents the most aggressive abstraction of infrastructure: you write functions, define triggers, and the cloud provider handles everything else, including provisioning servers, scaling to demand, applying security patches, and managing availability. You pay only for the compute time your functions consume, measured in milliseconds, making serverless extremely cost-effective for intermittent workloads. AWS Lambda, the leading serverless compute service, executes your code in response to events from over 200 AWS services: an HTTP request via API Gateway, a file upload to S3, a message in SQS, a change in DynamoDB. Event-driven architecture connects these serverless functions into scalable, loosely-coupled systems where each component reacts to events rather than calling other components directly. This session teaches you to build serverless applications that leverage the full power of event-driven architecture while understanding the trade-offs, particularly cold start latency, execution time limits, and the challenge of maintaining state, that determine when serverless is and is not the right choice.
key_concepts: AWS Lambda: function-as-a-service with automatic scaling from zero to thousands of concurrent executions
Lambda execution model: cold start (initialize runtime + code) vs warm start (reuse existing container), 15-minute max execution time
API Gateway: HTTP endpoint that triggers Lambda functions, with request validation, throttling, and API key management
Event sources: S3 object events, SQS message arrival, DynamoDB Streams, CloudWatch scheduled events, SNS notifications
Event-driven decoupling: components communicate through events, not direct calls, enabling independent scaling and deployment
SQS: fully managed message queue for asynchronous processing with at-least-once delivery and dead-letter queues
SNS: pub/sub messaging for fan-out to multiple subscribers (Lambda, SQS, email, HTTP endpoints)
Step Functions: orchestrating multi-step serverless workflows with branching, error handling, and retry logic
Provisioned concurrency: pre-warming Lambda containers to eliminate cold start latency for latency-sensitive endpoints
Serverless trade-offs: no server management and pay-per-use versus cold starts, 15-minute limit, no persistent connections, and vendor lock-in
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a serverless image processing pipeline: S3 upload triggers a Lambda function that validates the image, sends a message to SQS, a second Lambda consumes the message and resizes the image to multiple dimensions, stores results back in S3, writes metadata to DynamoDB, and sends a completion notification via SNS, all with dead-letter queue handling for failures
setup_requirements: AWS account with Lambda, S3, SQS, SNS, DynamoDB, and API Gateway permissions
AWS SAM CLI installed for local testing and deployment
Node.js or Python runtime for Lambda functions
Sample images for testing the pipeline

review.value: Cold starts add 100ms-10s of latency depending on runtime (Python/Node fastest, Java/C# slowest) and package size. Mitigate with: provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive paths, keeping deployment packages small, initializing SDK clients outside the handler function, and choosing lighter runtimes

Lambda guarantees at-least-once execution, meaning your function may run multiple times for the same event. Design handlers to be idempotent: processing the same event twice produces the same result. Use unique event IDs and check-before-write patterns to prevent duplicate processing

When a Lambda function fails after retries (default 2 for async), the event is sent to a dead-letter queue (SQS or SNS). Monitor the DLQ for failed events, investigate root causes, and replay events after fixing the issue. Without a DLQ, failed events are silently discarded

Serverless for: event-driven workloads, intermittent traffic, rapid prototyping, and when operational simplicity is paramount. Containers for: long-running processes, consistent latency requirements, workloads exceeding 15 minutes, and when you need persistent connections or local filesystem state

learning_objectives: Decompose monolithic applications into microservices with clear bounded contexts
Implement inter-service communication patterns including synchronous REST and asynchronous messaging
Design for failure with circuit breakers, retries, timeouts, and graceful degradation

lecture_notes.introduction: Microservices architecture decomposes a monolithic application into small, independently deployable services, each owning its own data and business logic. This decomposition enables teams to develop, deploy, and scale services independently, choosing the best technology for each service's specific requirements. However, microservices are not free: they trade the complexity of a large monolithic codebase for the complexity of a distributed system. Network communication between services introduces latency, partial failure modes, and consistency challenges that do not exist in a monolith. This session teaches you when microservices are appropriate (and when a monolith is better), how to identify service boundaries using domain-driven design concepts, how to implement communication between services, and critically, how to design for the inevitable failures that occur in distributed systems using patterns like circuit breakers, retries with exponential backoff, and graceful degradation.
key_concepts: Microservice principles: single responsibility, independent deployment, decentralized data management, failure isolation
Bounded contexts: identifying service boundaries based on business domain boundaries, not technical layers
Synchronous communication: REST APIs and gRPC for request/response interactions between services
Asynchronous communication: message queues (SQS) and event buses (EventBridge) for decoupled interactions
API Gateway pattern: single entry point that routes, authenticates, and rate-limits requests to internal services
Circuit breaker: automatically stopping calls to a failing service to prevent cascade failures, with half-open state for recovery detection
Service mesh: infrastructure layer (Istio, App Mesh) that handles inter-service networking, security, and observability
Saga pattern: managing distributed transactions across services without two-phase commit using compensating transactions
Database per service: each service owns its data store, no direct database sharing, data accessed only through service APIs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Decompose a monolithic e-commerce application into three microservices (product catalog, order management, payment processing): deploy each on separate ECS Fargate tasks, implement REST communication with circuit breakers using resilience patterns, connect the order and payment services via SQS for asynchronous processing, and demonstrate failure handling when the payment service goes down
setup_requirements: AWS account with ECS, SQS, and API Gateway permissions
Docker images for each microservice
Service mesh or client-side resilience library
Load testing tool for failure scenario simulation

review.value: Start with a well-structured monolith and extract microservices only when there is a clear need: different scaling requirements, different deployment cadences, or team ownership boundaries. Premature decomposition creates distributed monolith complexity without the benefits

Closed (normal operation, all calls pass through), Open (calls fail immediately without contacting the service, protecting against cascade failure), Half-Open (allows a limited number of test calls to check if the service has recovered). This pattern prevents a single failing service from consuming all resources of its callers

In a monolith, a database transaction ensures atomicity. In microservices, operations span multiple databases. The saga pattern coordinates multi-service transactions through a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions for rollback. This provides eventual consistency, not ACID guarantees

Each microservice owns its database and no other service accesses it directly. Data sharing happens through APIs. This enables independent schema evolution, technology selection, and scaling. The cost is data duplication and eventual consistency for cross-service queries

learning_objectives: Design CI/CD pipelines that build, test, and deploy cloud applications automatically
Implement infrastructure-as-code with Terraform for reproducible cloud environments
Configure blue-green and canary deployment strategies for zero-downtime releases
readings: Terraform Documentation: Getting Started with AWS

lecture_notes.introduction: Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment form the backbone of modern cloud operations, automating the path from code commit to production deployment. Without CI/CD, deployments are manual, error-prone, and infrequent, leading to large, risky releases. With CI/CD, every code change is automatically built, tested, and deployed through a pipeline of stages, catching errors early and enabling multiple production deployments per day with confidence. Infrastructure as Code (IaC) extends this automation to the infrastructure itself: your cloud environment is defined in version-controlled Terraform files that can be reviewed, tested, and applied just like application code. This eliminates manual console clicking, prevents configuration drift between environments, and enables disaster recovery by recreating infrastructure from code. This session covers the complete CI/CD pipeline from source control through build and test to deployment, focusing on cloud-native deployment strategies that enable zero-downtime releases and easy rollback.
key_concepts: Continuous Integration: automatically building and testing code on every commit, failing fast on errors
Continuous Deployment: automatically deploying passing builds to production without manual intervention
Pipeline stages: source (Git), build (compile, package), test (unit, integration, security), deploy (staging, production)
Infrastructure as Code: Terraform HCL for declaring cloud resources, with plan, apply, and destroy lifecycle
Terraform state: remote state storage in S3 with DynamoDB locking for team collaboration without conflicts
Terraform modules: reusable infrastructure components parameterized for different environments (dev, staging, prod)
Blue-green deployment: two identical environments (blue and green); deploy to inactive, test, switch traffic, keep old as rollback
Canary deployment: route a small percentage of traffic to the new version, monitor metrics, gradually increase if healthy
Rollback strategy: automated rollback when health checks fail, with clear criteria for success and failure determination
GitOps: Git as the single source of truth for both application code and infrastructure, with reconciliation loops
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete CI/CD pipeline: write Terraform to deploy a VPC, ECS cluster, and ALB, configure GitHub Actions to build Docker images, run tests, push to ECR, and deploy to ECS with a blue-green strategy, then trigger a deployment and watch traffic shift from the old version to the new version with automatic rollback on health check failure
setup_requirements: GitHub repository with Actions enabled
Terraform installed with AWS provider configured
AWS account with S3 bucket for Terraform state
ECS cluster for deployment target

review.value: Always run terraform plan and review the output before terraform apply. The plan shows exactly what resources will be created, modified, or destroyed. In CI/CD, the plan output should be reviewed as part of a pull request before the apply runs automatically on merge

Blue-green is simpler: all traffic switches at once, easy rollback by switching back. Canary is safer: gradual rollout catches issues that only manifest under partial load. Use blue-green for small services with good test coverage; canary for high-traffic services where full-traffic testing is risky

CI/CD pipelines have broad access to deploy code and infrastructure. Secure them: use OIDC federation instead of long-lived AWS credentials, restrict pipeline permissions with least-privilege IAM roles, require approval gates for production deployments, and audit all pipeline executions

Staging must mirror production as closely as possible: same Terraform modules with different variables, same container images, same database engine version. Differences between environments are the primary cause of 'it works in staging but fails in production' incidents

learning_objectives: Implement centralized logging with CloudWatch Logs and structured log formats
Design monitoring dashboards and alerts using CloudWatch Metrics and alarms
Build distributed tracing across microservices using X-Ray for request flow visibility
readings: AWS CloudWatch Documentation: Monitoring and Observability
Google SRE Book, Ch. 6: Monitoring Distributed Systems

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot fix what you cannot see. In cloud environments where applications span dozens of services across multiple availability zones, observability, the ability to understand the internal state of your system from its external outputs, is essential for operational reliability. When a user reports that the checkout page is slow, you need to determine within minutes whether the problem is in the web server, the application code, the database, the network, or an external API. The three pillars of observability are metrics (quantitative measurements over time), logs (detailed records of events), and traces (the path of a request through multiple services). This session covers how to instrument your cloud applications with all three pillars using AWS-native tools: CloudWatch Metrics for real-time dashboards and alerting, CloudWatch Logs for centralized log aggregation and search, and X-Ray for distributed request tracing across microservices. You will learn to design alerts that notify you of real problems without drowning you in false positives.
key_concepts: Three pillars of observability: metrics (what is happening), logs (why it is happening), traces (where it is happening in the request flow)
CloudWatch Metrics: time-series data points (CPU utilization, request count, error rate) with configurable resolution and retention
CloudWatch Alarms: threshold-based alerts (CPU > 80% for 5 minutes) that trigger SNS notifications or Auto Scaling actions
CloudWatch Logs: centralized log aggregation with log groups, log streams, retention policies, and metric filters
Structured logging: JSON-formatted logs with consistent fields (timestamp, level, request_id, service) for machine parsing and querying
CloudWatch Dashboards: custom visualizations combining metrics, logs, and alarms for operational awareness
AWS X-Ray: distributed tracing that maps request flow across services with latency breakdown per segment
Service Level Indicators (SLIs): specific metrics that measure user-facing service quality (latency p99, error rate, availability)
Service Level Objectives (SLOs): target values for SLIs (p99 latency < 500ms, availability > 99.9%) that drive alerting thresholds
Alert fatigue prevention: alert only on user-impacting symptoms, not every internal metric, with appropriate severity levels and escalation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Instrument a microservices application with full observability: configure structured JSON logging to CloudWatch Logs with correlation IDs, create custom CloudWatch Metrics for business events (orders placed, payments processed), set up X-Ray tracing across three services to visualize request flow, build a CloudWatch Dashboard combining all signals, and configure alarms that trigger PagerDuty notifications when the error rate exceeds the SLO
setup_requirements: AWS account with CloudWatch, X-Ray, and SNS permissions
Application instrumented with AWS SDK and X-Ray SDK
CloudWatch agent installed on EC2 instances or ECS task definitions
PagerDuty or email endpoint for alarm notifications

review.value: Unstructured logs (free-text messages) are nearly impossible to query at scale. Structured logs (JSON with consistent fields) enable CloudWatch Insights queries like: filter errorCode = 500 | stats count() by service, endpoint | sort count desc. This turns logs from debugging tools into analytics tools

Generate a unique request ID at the API gateway and propagate it through all downstream service calls via HTTP headers. Include this ID in every log entry. When debugging an issue, search for the correlation ID to see the complete request journey across all services in chronological order

USE (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) for infrastructure metrics: is the CPU busy, are requests queuing, are things failing. RED (Rate, Errors, Duration) for service metrics: how many requests, how many fail, how long do they take. Monitor both for complete visibility

Alert on user-impacting symptoms (error rate > 1%, latency p99 > 2s) not on causes (CPU > 80%). High CPU with happy users is not an incident. Normal CPU with elevated errors is an incident. Cause-based alerts create noise that leads to alert fatigue and missed real incidents

learning_objectives: Implement IAM policies following the principle of least privilege with conditions and boundaries
Configure encryption at rest and in transit for all data stores and communication channels
Automate security compliance checking with AWS Config rules and Security Hub

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud security is not a feature you add after deployment; it is a design principle woven into every architectural decision from the first line of Terraform code. Cloud misconfiguration is the number one cause of cloud security breaches, responsible for more incidents than sophisticated attacks because it is far easier to accidentally leave an S3 bucket public than to exploit a zero-day vulnerability. The scale and complexity of cloud environments, with hundreds of services, thousands of IAM policies, and constantly changing infrastructure, make manual security review impossible. Effective cloud security requires automation: policies that prevent insecure configurations before they are deployed, continuous compliance monitoring that detects drift, and automated remediation that fixes violations without human intervention. This session covers the core security controls for AWS environments: IAM for identity and access management, encryption for data protection, network security at the VPC level, and automated compliance checking that ensures your security posture remains strong as your infrastructure evolves.
key_concepts: IAM principals: users, groups, roles, and service-linked roles with different trust and permission models
IAM policies: JSON documents specifying Allow or Deny for specific Actions on specific Resources with optional Conditions
Least privilege principle: every IAM principal should have only the minimum permissions required for its specific function
Permission boundaries: limiting the maximum permissions that an IAM entity can receive, even if broader policies are attached
Encryption at rest: S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS), RDS encryption, EBS encryption using AWS KMS keys
Encryption in transit: TLS certificates on ALB, enforcing HTTPS, VPN for hybrid connectivity
AWS KMS: centralized key management with automatic rotation, access policies, and audit trail via CloudTrail
AWS Config: continuous compliance monitoring with rules that detect and alert on configuration violations
Security Hub: aggregates findings from Config, GuardDuty, Inspector, and third-party tools into a unified security dashboard
CloudTrail: audit log of every API call in your AWS account for forensic analysis and compliance evidence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement defense-in-depth for a web application: create least-privilege IAM roles for the application and CI/CD pipeline, enable S3 bucket encryption and block public access, configure RDS encryption with a customer-managed KMS key, set up AWS Config rules to detect public S3 buckets and unencrypted EBS volumes, and enable Security Hub with CIS benchmark checks
setup_requirements: AWS account with IAM, KMS, Config, and Security Hub permissions
Terraform files for infrastructure-as-code security configuration
AWS CLI for IAM policy testing with iam simulate-principal-policy
CloudTrail enabled for audit logging

review.value: Start with zero permissions and add only what is needed. Use IAM Access Analyzer to identify unused permissions and tighten policies. Never use Action: * or Resource: * in production. Review IAM policies in pull requests just like application code

Block all public access at the account level. Enable server-side encryption with KMS. Enable versioning for data recovery. Enable access logging to an audit bucket. Use bucket policies to restrict access to specific IAM roles. These settings should be in your Terraform baseline, not applied manually

Never store secrets in code, environment variables, or configuration files. Use AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation for database credentials and API keys. Reference secrets by ARN in your application code, and grant IAM permission to retrieve only the specific secrets each service needs

Manual security reviews do not scale. Automate in this order: (1) preventive controls in Terraform that block insecure configurations, (2) detective controls with Config rules that alert on drift, (3) responsive controls with Lambda that auto-remediate common violations (close public S3 buckets automatically)

learning_objectives: Analyze cloud spending using Cost Explorer and implement tagging strategies for cost allocation
Apply right-sizing, reserved instances, and spot instances to reduce compute costs by 40-70%
Establish FinOps practices for continuous cost optimization as a shared organizational responsibility

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud computing shifts IT spending from predictable capital expenditure to variable operational expenditure, which creates both opportunity and risk. The opportunity is paying only for what you use; the risk is that without active cost management, cloud bills grow unchecked as teams provision resources and forget about them, choose expensive instance types by default, or leave development environments running 24/7. Cloud cost optimization is not a one-time exercise; it is an ongoing discipline that the FinOps community has formalized into a practice combining systems, best practices, and cultural change. Organizations that ignore cloud costs typically overspend by 30-50% compared to an optimized architecture. This session teaches the FinOps framework for managing cloud costs: establishing visibility through tagging and cost allocation, right-sizing resources based on actual utilization data, leveraging commitment discounts (reserved instances, savings plans) for predictable workloads, using spot instances for fault-tolerant batch processing, and implementing budgets and alerts that catch cost anomalies before they become budget-busting surprises.
key_concepts: FinOps lifecycle: Inform (visibility and allocation), Optimize (recommendations and actions), Operate (continuous governance)
Cost Explorer: AWS tool for analyzing spending patterns by service, account, tag, and time period with forecasting
Resource tagging strategy: consistent tags (team, project, environment, cost-center) for cost allocation and showback/chargeback
Right-sizing: analyzing instance utilization and recommending smaller instance types where CPU/memory is underutilized
Reserved Instances and Savings Plans: committing to 1-year or 3-year usage for 30-60% discount on on-demand pricing
Spot instances: using spare EC2 capacity at 60-90% discount for fault-tolerant, flexible workloads with interruption handling
AWS Budgets: setting spending thresholds with email/SNS alerts when actual or forecasted spend exceeds the budget
Idle resource detection: identifying and terminating unused EC2 instances, unattached EBS volumes, idle load balancers, and unused Elastic IPs
Storage cost optimization: S3 Intelligent-Tiering, EBS volume type selection, RDS reserved instances, DynamoDB on-demand vs provisioned
Architectural cost optimization: serverless for intermittent workloads, auto-scaling to match demand, multi-tier storage, caching to reduce compute
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze an AWS account's spending using Cost Explorer, identify the top cost drivers, right-size overprovisioned EC2 instances based on CloudWatch CPU utilization data, calculate savings from converting on-demand instances to Savings Plans, find and terminate idle resources (stopped instances with attached EBS, unused Elastic IPs), implement a tagging policy and budget alerts, and estimate total monthly savings achieved
setup_requirements: AWS account with Cost Explorer and Budgets enabled (requires billing access)
CloudWatch metrics for instance utilization analysis
AWS Pricing Calculator for cost comparison
Tagging policy document template

review.value: Pull 2 weeks of CloudWatch CPU and memory utilization. If average CPU is below 40% and peak is below 70%, the instance is overprovisioned. Recommend one size down. Repeat after resize. Right-sizing is iterative because workloads change and initial estimates are often wrong

Reserved Instances commit to a specific instance type in a specific region. Savings Plans commit to a dollar amount of compute usage regardless of instance type. Savings Plans offer more flexibility (change instance types, use Fargate or Lambda) while providing similar discounts. Prefer Savings Plans for evolving architectures

Development and staging environments rarely need to run 24/7. Scheduling them to run only during business hours (12 hours/day, 5 days/week) saves 64% compared to always-on. Use AWS Instance Scheduler or a simple Lambda function to start/stop instances on a schedule

AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses ML to identify unexpected spending spikes. Configure it to alert on anomalies above a dollar threshold. This catches accidental resource creation, misconfigured auto-scaling, and price increases before they accumulate into a large bill

class_schedule.topic: Final Project Presentations and Cloud Architecture Review
learning_objectives: Present a complete cloud-native application architecture demonstrating all course concepts
Evaluate peer architectures for security, reliability, cost efficiency, and operational readiness
Synthesize cloud computing principles into an architecture decision framework
readings: Cloud Architecture Patterns: Using Microsoft Azure by Bill Wilder, Ch. 1-3
The Phoenix Project by Kim, Behr, and Spafford (review)

lecture_notes.introduction: The final week of this course brings together all the cloud computing concepts covered over the semester through project presentations and a comprehensive review of cloud architecture principles. Each student presents their cloud-native application, demonstrating not just that it works but that it is well-architected across all five pillars: operational excellence (monitoring, CI/CD, runbooks), security (IAM, encryption, compliance), reliability (multi-AZ, auto-scaling, failover), performance efficiency (right compute selection, caching, CDN), and cost optimization (right-sizing, reservations, tagging). Presentations are evaluated on architectural decision quality, infrastructure-as-code completeness, security posture, operational readiness, and the ability to articulate trade-offs. After presentations, we review the complete cloud architecture lifecycle and discuss emerging trends including edge computing, multi-cloud orchestration, platform engineering, and the impact of AI on cloud operations that are shaping the future of cloud computing.
key_concepts: Well-Architected Review: systematic evaluation of an architecture against AWS's five pillar framework
Architecture decision records: documenting what was decided, why, what alternatives were considered, and what trade-offs accepted
Disaster recovery strategies: backup and restore, pilot light, warm standby, multi-site active-active with RTO/RPO trade-offs
Cloud maturity model: from lift-and-shift through re-platform to cloud-native with increasing cloud benefit at each level
Platform engineering: building internal developer platforms that abstract cloud complexity for application teams
Edge computing: running compute at CDN edge locations (Lambda@Edge, CloudFront Functions) for ultra-low latency
AI/ML operations: SageMaker for model training, inference endpoints, and MLOps pipelines in the cloud
Sustainability pillar: minimizing carbon footprint through efficient resource usage, right-sizing, and region selection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through an exemplary final project architecture: a multi-tier web application on ECS Fargate with Aurora Serverless, ElastiCache, CloudFront CDN, complete Terraform IaC, GitHub Actions CI/CD, CloudWatch observability, IAM least-privilege, and a cost analysis showing optimization decisions
setup_requirements: Presentation slides or architecture diagram tool
Live AWS console access for demonstration
Terraform code repository for infrastructure review
Cost analysis spreadsheet or Cost Explorer screenshots

review.value: For every cloud service decision, evaluate: Does it meet the functional requirement? Is it the simplest option that works? What is the operational burden? What is the cost? What is the lock-in risk? What happens when it fails? Document the decision and rationale in an Architecture Decision Record

Operational Excellence: automate everything, monitor everything, plan for failure. Security: least privilege, encrypt everything, detect and respond. Reliability: multi-AZ, auto-scale, test failure scenarios. Performance: right compute, cache aggressively, measure latency. Cost: tag everything, right-size, commit where predictable

Cloud engineering roles span: cloud developer (building applications on cloud services), cloud architect (designing cloud solutions), DevOps/SRE (operating cloud infrastructure), cloud security engineer (securing cloud environments), FinOps practitioner (optimizing cloud costs). AWS certifications validate knowledge at each level

AWS launches over 2,000 new features per year. Cloud architecture best practices evolve continuously. Subscribe to provider blogs, attend re:Invent or equivalent conferences, maintain hands-on labs, and pursue certifications. The cloud engineer who stops learning falls behind within a year

questions.question: Explain the difference between horizontal scaling and vertical scaling. Which approach does cloud architecture prefer, and what architectural constraints does horizontal scaling impose on application design?
questions.answer: Vertical scaling (scale up) adds more CPU/RAM to an existing server. It is simple (no application changes needed) but has a hardware ceiling and creates a single point of failure. Horizontal scaling (scale out) adds more server instances behind a load balancer. It has no theoretical ceiling and provides redundancy. Cloud architecture prefers horizontal scaling because cloud instances are commodities designed to be added and removed elastically. However, horizontal scaling imposes constraints: (1) The application must be stateless or externalize state to a shared store (database, cache) so any instance can handle any request. (2) Sessions must use sticky sessions or external session stores. (3) File storage must use shared storage (S3, EFS) not local disk. (4) Database must handle concurrent connections from multiple instances.

rubric.key: Correctly defines both scaling types with trade-offs
Identifies horizontal as preferred in cloud with reasoning
Lists architectural constraints imposed by horizontal scaling

questions.question: Write a multi-stage Dockerfile for a Go web application that compiles the binary in a build stage and copies only the compiled binary to a minimal runtime stage. Explain why this approach reduces image size and improves security.
questions.answer:
# Build stage
FROM golang:1.22 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o /server ./cmd/server

# Runtime stage
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /server /server
USER nonroot:nonroot
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]

# Size reduction: The Go SDK image is ~800MB with compiler, standard library, and tools. The distroless image contains only the compiled binary (~10-20MB). Build tools are not shipped.
# Security improvement: The runtime image has no shell, no package manager, no OS utilities. An attacker who compromises the container cannot install tools, explore the filesystem, or escalate privileges. Running as nonroot prevents container escape via root.

rubric.key: Correct multi-stage build with separate build and runtime stages
Uses minimal runtime image (distroless or alpine) with nonroot user
Explains size reduction and security benefits

questions.question: A startup is choosing between running their web application on EC2 instances, ECS with Fargate, or AWS Lambda behind API Gateway. The application receives 100 requests per second during business hours and near-zero traffic overnight. Which compute service would you recommend and why?
questions.answer: Recommended: ECS with Fargate. Reasoning: (1) 100 req/s is moderate sustained load during business hours, which is a poor fit for Lambda due to consistent concurrency costs and potential cold start latency impacting user experience. Lambda excels for intermittent, event-driven workloads, not sustained HTTP serving. (2) EC2 requires managing OS patches, instance health, and capacity planning. The overnight idle period wastes money on running instances unless you implement complex scheduling. (3) Fargate provides container-based deployment without managing servers. Combined with auto-scaling, it scales down to minimal tasks overnight (near-zero traffic means near-zero cost) and scales up during business hours. You pay only for running tasks. (4) Fargate gives more control than Lambda (longer execution, persistent connections, larger memory) without the operational burden of EC2. Alternative consideration: If overnight truly reaches zero traffic and cold starts are acceptable, Lambda could work and would cost nothing during idle periods.

rubric.key: Recommends Fargate with clear justification
Explains why Lambda is suboptimal for sustained 100 req/s
Explains why EC2 wastes money during overnight idle

questions.question: Explain the difference between a Kubernetes Deployment and a Kubernetes Service. Why are both needed to run a web application in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: A Deployment manages the desired state of pods: how many replicas to run, which container image to use, and how to perform rolling updates. It creates and manages ReplicaSets that maintain the desired pod count, replacing failed pods and orchestrating version updates. A Service provides a stable network endpoint (IP address and DNS name) for accessing a set of pods identified by label selectors. Pods are ephemeral: they get new IP addresses when recreated. Without a Service, clients would need to track individual pod IPs. Both are needed because: the Deployment ensures the right number of healthy pods are running (compute concern), while the Service ensures traffic reaches those pods through a stable address (networking concern). The Deployment can scale pods, update versions, and replace failures independently of the Service. The Service automatically updates its endpoints as pods come and go.

rubric.key: Correctly defines Deployment as managing pod lifecycle and desired state
Correctly defines Service as providing stable network endpoint
Explains why both are needed with separation of concerns

questions.question: Design the storage architecture for an e-commerce application on AWS. Specify which AWS storage/database service you would use for: product catalog, user sessions, product images, order history, and shopping cart. Justify each choice.
questions.answer: Product catalog: RDS Aurora PostgreSQL. Relational data with complex queries (filter by category, price range, search by name), ACID transactions for inventory updates, read replicas for scaling read-heavy browsing traffic. User sessions: ElastiCache Redis. Sub-millisecond access for session data looked up on every request. TTL-based expiration for automatic session cleanup. Redis persistence for session survival across cache restarts. Product images: S3 Standard. Unlimited object storage for images of varying sizes. CloudFront CDN in front for global low-latency delivery. Lifecycle policy to move rarely accessed old product images to S3 Infrequent Access. Order history: DynamoDB. Key-value access pattern (get orders by user_id). Unlimited scale for growing order history. On-demand capacity handles spiky traffic (sales events). Global tables for multi-region if needed. Shopping cart: DynamoDB or ElastiCache Redis. DynamoDB if cart must persist across sessions (abandoned cart recovery). Redis if cart is session-scoped and speed is paramount. For most e-commerce, DynamoDB with TTL for automatic abandoned cart cleanup.

rubric.key: Appropriate service selection for each data type with justification
Considers access patterns (relational queries vs key-value lookups)
Includes performance and scaling considerations

questions.question: Explain why the following IAM policy is dangerous and rewrite it following the principle of least privilege for a Lambda function that reads from a specific S3 bucket and writes to a specific DynamoDB table.

rubric.key: Identifies the danger of Action:* Resource:*
Restricts actions to only required API calls per service
Restricts resources to specific ARNs, not wildcards

questions.question: Design a VPC architecture for a web application that requires high availability, database security, and internet access for application servers to call external APIs. Draw the architecture in text and explain the purpose of each component.
questions.answer:
Architecture (2 AZs):
VPC: 10.0.0.0/16

AZ-1: Public subnet 10.0.1.0/24 (ALB, NAT Gateway)
       Private subnet 10.0.3.0/24 (App servers)
       Private subnet 10.0.5.0/24 (Database)

AZ-2: Public subnet 10.0.2.0/24 (ALB)
       Private subnet 10.0.4.0/24 (App servers)
       Private subnet 10.0.6.0/24 (Database)

Components:
- Internet Gateway: allows inbound internet traffic to reach ALB in public subnets
- ALB (public subnets): distributes HTTPS traffic to app servers, terminates TLS
- NAT Gateway (public subnet AZ-1): allows app servers in private subnets to make outbound internet requests (external APIs, package updates) without being directly reachable from internet
- App servers (private app subnets): not directly internet-accessible, receive traffic only from ALB security group
- Database RDS Multi-AZ (private DB subnets): isolated from both internet and app subnets except on port 5432, primary in AZ-1 with synchronous standby in AZ-2
- Security groups: ALB allows inbound 443 from internet; App allows inbound from ALB SG only; DB allows inbound 5432 from App SG only

rubric.key: Multi-AZ design with public and private subnets
Correct placement of components (ALB in public, app and DB in private)
NAT Gateway for outbound and security group chain explained

questions.question: A Lambda function processes S3 upload events but occasionally processes the same file twice, creating duplicate records in DynamoDB. Explain why this happens and how to make the function idempotent.
questions.answer: Why duplicates occur: Lambda guarantees at-least-once delivery for asynchronous event sources like S3. If the function succeeds but Lambda fails to record the success (e.g., the function timeout occurs after writing to DynamoDB but before returning), Lambda retries the invocation. The function processes the same S3 event again, creating a duplicate record. Additionally, S3 event notifications can occasionally deliver duplicate events for the same object. Making it idempotent: (1) Use the S3 object key + version ID (or ETag) as a unique identifier. (2) Before processing, check DynamoDB for an existing record with that identifier using a conditional write: put the item with ConditionExpression: 'attribute_not_exists(objectKey)'. If the item already exists, the condition fails and the duplicate is skipped. (3) Alternatively, use a DynamoDB transaction or an idempotency table that tracks processed event IDs with a TTL for cleanup. The key principle: the function must produce the same result regardless of how many times it executes for the same event.

rubric.key: Explains at-least-once delivery causing duplicate invocations
Proposes conditional write or idempotency check before processing
Uses S3 object key + version as unique identifier

questions.question: Compare S3 Standard, S3 Intelligent-Tiering, S3 Glacier, and S3 Glacier Deep Archive. For each, give one specific use case where that storage class is the optimal choice.
questions.answer: S3 Standard: Frequently accessed data with low latency requirements. Use case: serving product images for an e-commerce site through CloudFront. Access is frequent and unpredictable; any retrieval delay would impact user experience. Cost: highest storage price, no retrieval fee. S3 Intelligent-Tiering: Data with unknown or changing access patterns. Use case: application log files that are queried heavily in the first week but rarely after 30 days. Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves data between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on actual usage, optimizing cost without manual lifecycle rules. Small monitoring fee per object. S3 Glacier: Archival data that may need retrieval within hours. Use case: medical records that must be retained for 7 years by regulation but are rarely accessed. Retrieval takes 1-5 minutes (expedited) to 3-5 hours (standard). Very low storage cost. S3 Glacier Deep Archive: Long-term archival with rare retrieval needs. Use case: financial audit records retained for 10+ years. Retrieval takes 12-48 hours. Lowest storage cost of any AWS service ($0.00099/GB/month). Acceptable when data is almost never retrieved.

rubric.key: Correctly describes each storage class characteristics
Appropriate use case for each class
Mentions cost and retrieval time trade-offs

questions.question: Explain the circuit breaker pattern in microservices. Describe all three states, when transitions occur, and what happens to requests in each state. Why is this pattern critical in distributed systems?
questions.answer: The circuit breaker prevents cascade failures when a downstream service is unresponsive. Three states: Closed (normal): all requests pass through to the downstream service. Failures are counted. When failures exceed a threshold (e.g., 5 failures in 10 seconds), the circuit trips to Open. Open: all requests fail immediately without contacting the downstream service. A timer starts. This protects the calling service from wasting threads/connections waiting for timeouts. After the timer expires, transitions to Half-Open. Half-Open: a limited number of test requests are allowed through. If they succeed, the circuit resets to Closed. If they fail, the circuit returns to Open. Critical because: Without a circuit breaker, when Service B goes down, Service A's threads block on timeouts, exhausting its thread pool, causing Service A to also become unresponsive, causing Service C (which calls A) to fail, and so on. One failing service cascades into a complete system outage. The circuit breaker cuts the chain, letting healthy services continue operating with degraded functionality.

rubric.key: Correctly describes all three states and transitions
Explains immediate failure in Open state protecting resources
Describes cascade failure prevention in distributed systems

questions.question: You run terraform plan and see that Terraform wants to destroy and recreate your RDS database instance because you changed the engine version. This would cause data loss. How do you safely upgrade the database version without losing data?
questions.answer: This occurs because Terraform sees the engine version change as a force-replacement attribute. Safe upgrade approach: (1) First, take a manual snapshot of the current database as a safety net: aws rds create-db-snapshot. (2) Check if the RDS engine supports in-place upgrade for your version change (e.g., PostgreSQL 14 to 15). If supported, add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the RDS resource temporarily to prevent accidental destruction. (3) Modify the Terraform configuration to use the new engine version and set apply_method = 'immediate' or schedule during maintenance window. (4) Run terraform plan again. If Terraform still shows destroy/create instead of modify-in-place, this version jump requires a manual upgrade: perform the upgrade through the AWS console or CLI (aws rds modify-db-instance --engine-version), then update the Terraform state with terraform import or terraform state commands to reflect the new version. (5) Verify the upgrade completed successfully and data is intact. (6) Remove the prevent_destroy lifecycle rule. Never let Terraform destroy a production database without verifying data backup exists.

rubric.key: Takes a snapshot before any changes as safety net
Uses lifecycle prevent_destroy to prevent accidental destruction
Describes in-place upgrade path or manual upgrade with state sync
Verifies data integrity after upgrade
