questions.question: Design an alerting strategy for a production web application. Specify three alerts you would create, including the metric, threshold, evaluation period, and action for each. Explain why you chose symptom-based alerts over cause-based alerts.
questions.answer: Alert 1: Error Rate. Metric: 5xx error count / total request count. Threshold: > 1% for 3 consecutive 1-minute periods. Action: Page on-call engineer via PagerDuty (high severity). Rationale: Users are experiencing failures. Alert 2: Latency. Metric: p99 response time from ALB. Threshold: > 2 seconds for 5 consecutive 1-minute periods. Action: Page on-call engineer (medium severity). Rationale: User experience is degraded even though requests succeed. Alert 3: Availability. Metric: Route 53 health check failure. Threshold: Health check fails 3 consecutive times (30 seconds apart). Action: Trigger DNS failover to backup region and page on-call (critical severity). Rationale: The entire application is unreachable. Symptom-based over cause-based: CPU at 90% is a cause; it might not affect users if the server is handling load fine. Error rate > 1% is a symptom; users are definitely impacted. Cause-based alerts (high CPU, low disk, many connections) generate noise because not every cause leads to user impact. Symptom-based alerts trigger only when users are affected, reducing alert fatigue and ensuring every alert demands investigation.

rubric.key: Three well-defined alerts with metric, threshold, period, and action
Alerts cover error rate, latency, and availability
Explains symptom-based over cause-based reasoning

questions.question: An AWS account has a monthly bill of $15,000. Cost Explorer shows: EC2 on-demand instances ($6,000), RDS on-demand ($3,000), S3 Standard ($2,000), data transfer ($2,000), and other services ($2,000). The EC2 and RDS workloads are steady-state. Propose a cost optimization plan with estimated savings for each recommendation.
questions.answer: Optimization plan: 1. EC2 Savings Plans ($6,000 -> ~$2,400): Convert steady-state EC2 to 1-year Compute Savings Plan for ~60% discount. Estimated savings: $3,600/month. 2. RDS Reserved Instances ($3,000 -> ~$1,500): Purchase 1-year RDS reserved instances for ~50% discount on steady-state databases. Estimated savings: $1,500/month. 3. S3 Storage Optimization ($2,000 -> ~$1,200): Analyze access patterns with S3 Analytics. Move infrequently accessed data to S3-IA (60% cheaper) or enable Intelligent-Tiering. Estimated savings: $800/month. 4. Data Transfer ($2,000 -> ~$1,400): Place CloudFront in front of the application to cache static content at edge (data transfer from CloudFront is cheaper than from EC2). Compress responses. Use VPC endpoints for S3/DynamoDB to eliminate NAT Gateway data processing charges. Estimated savings: $600/month. 5. Right-sizing ($500 additional): Analyze EC2 CPU/memory utilization and downsize over-provisioned instances. Total estimated savings: ~$6,500/month (43% reduction), bringing the bill from $15,000 to ~$8,500.

rubric.key: Recommends Savings Plans or Reserved Instances for steady-state compute
Addresses S3 storage class optimization
Includes data transfer optimization and right-sizing
Provides realistic savings estimates with reasoning

questions.question: Describe three common cloud security misconfigurations that lead to data breaches and explain how to prevent each with automated controls.
questions.answer: 1. Public S3 buckets: Misconfigured bucket policies or ACLs that allow anonymous read access. Thousands of breaches have resulted from publicly exposed S3 buckets containing customer data. Prevention: Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level (applies to all buckets). Deploy an AWS Config rule (s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited) that detects and alerts on any public bucket. Create a Lambda auto-remediation that immediately blocks public access when detected. 2. Overly permissive security groups: Security groups with inbound 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22 (SSH) or 3389 (RDP) expose instances to brute-force attacks from the entire internet. Prevention: AWS Config rule (restricted-ssh) detects open SSH. Use Systems Manager Session Manager for shell access instead of SSH, eliminating the need for port 22 entirely. Terraform modules should enforce no 0.0.0.0/0 inbound rules by default. 3. Exposed credentials: AWS access keys hardcoded in source code, committed to Git repositories, or stored in environment variables. Prevention: Use IAM roles for EC2/Lambda/ECS instead of access keys. Enable AWS Secrets Manager for application credentials. Scan repositories with git-secrets or GitHub secret scanning. AWS Config rule (access-keys-rotated) enforces key rotation if keys must exist.

rubric.key: Three distinct, realistic misconfigurations identified
Automated prevention control for each (Config rules, Lambda remediation)
Practical and implementable recommendations

questions.question: Explain the difference between blue-green deployment and canary deployment. For each, describe how rollback works and when you would choose one over the other.
questions.answer: Blue-green deployment: Two identical production environments (blue and green). Deploy the new version to the inactive environment, test it, then switch 100% of traffic from active to inactive. Rollback: instantly switch traffic back to the previous environment (it is still running with the old version). The entire switch is a DNS or load balancer change, completing in seconds. Choose when: you have good automated test coverage, the service handles moderate traffic, you want simple all-or-nothing deployment with instant rollback, and you can afford running two environments during deployment. Canary deployment: Route a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 5%) to the new version. Monitor error rate, latency, and business metrics. If healthy, gradually increase (10%, 25%, 50%, 100%). If unhealthy, route all traffic back to the old version. Rollback: route the canary traffic back to the old version. Only the small percentage of users on the canary were affected. Choose when: the service handles high traffic where a full-traffic switch is risky, you need to validate under real production load that tests cannot simulate, or you want to minimize blast radius of potential issues. The canary catches problems that only manifest under partial real-world conditions.

rubric.key: Correctly describes both deployment strategies
Explains rollback mechanism for each
Provides clear criteria for choosing one over the other

major_assignments.title: Containerized Application Deployment
major_assignments.description: Containerize a multi-tier web application with Docker, deploy it to a Kubernetes cluster, and configure auto-scaling, monitoring, and networking. The application must include a web frontend, an API backend, and a database, each in its own container with proper resource limits, health checks, and inter-service communication.
objectives: Write production-quality Dockerfiles with multi-stage builds and security best practices
Deploy a multi-container application to Kubernetes with Deployments, Services, and ConfigMaps
Configure Horizontal Pod Autoscaler and test scaling behavior under load
Implement Kubernetes networking with proper service discovery and ingress configuration
deliverables: Dockerfiles for each application tier with multi-stage builds
Kubernetes manifest files (YAML) for all resources
Load test report showing auto-scaling behavior with metrics
Architecture document (3 pages) explaining container and orchestration decisions

criteria: Multi-stage Dockerfiles producing minimal, secure runtime images
Kubernetes manifests include resource limits, health probes, and proper labeling
HPA correctly scales based on CPU with appropriate min/max bounds
Inter-service communication uses Kubernetes Service DNS, not hardcoded IPs
Architecture diagram clearly shows all components and their relationships
Load test results presented with clear graphs showing scaling events
Container image sizes are optimized (below 100MB for application images)
Kubernetes resource organization follows namespace and labeling conventions
Docker layer caching optimized for fast rebuilds on code changes
Kubernetes rolling update strategy configured for zero-downtime deployment
Resource requests and limits are right-sized based on actual usage
Health checks detect real application health, not just port availability
Architecture document explains containerization and orchestration decisions
README includes complete build, push, and deploy instructions
Dockerfile comments explain each instruction's purpose
Load test methodology and results are well-documented
Production-quality containerized multi-tier application
Complete Kubernetes deployment with auto-scaling and networking
Zero-downtime deployment with proper health checks
Comprehensive documentation of architecture and testing

major_assignments.title: Serverless Event-Driven Architecture
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a serverless event-driven system using AWS Lambda, API Gateway, SQS, SNS, DynamoDB, and S3. The system must process events asynchronously, handle failures with dead-letter queues, and include comprehensive monitoring and alerting.
objectives: Build Lambda functions triggered by multiple event sources with proper error handling
Design asynchronous processing pipelines using SQS with dead-letter queue patterns
Implement DynamoDB access patterns with proper partition key design and conditional writes
Configure CloudWatch monitoring with custom metrics and operational alarms
deliverables: SAM or CDK template defining all serverless resources as infrastructure-as-code
Lambda function source code with unit tests
Architecture diagram showing event flow, including failure paths and DLQ handling
Monitoring dashboard configuration with alarms for error rate, latency, and DLQ depth

criteria: Lambda functions handle multiple event sources with idempotent processing
SQS queues configured with appropriate visibility timeout, retry count, and DLQ
DynamoDB table design supports required access patterns with efficient partition keys
API Gateway configured with request validation, throttling, and error responses
Architecture diagram clearly shows event flow including happy path and failure paths
API documentation covers all endpoints with request/response examples
CloudWatch dashboard provides operational visibility into all system components
Error handling provides meaningful error messages to API consumers
Lambda cold start minimized through package optimization and provisioned concurrency where needed
DynamoDB access patterns avoid hot partitions and unnecessary scans
SQS batch processing used where appropriate for throughput
Lambda timeout and memory configured appropriately for each function's requirements
Infrastructure-as-code template is complete and deployable
Architecture document explains event flow and failure handling strategy
DynamoDB access patterns documented with table design rationale
Monitoring and alerting strategy documented with escalation procedures
Complete serverless event-driven architecture with multiple AWS services
Robust error handling with DLQ and idempotent processing
Efficient DynamoDB design with appropriate access patterns
Comprehensive monitoring with actionable alerts

major_assignments.title: Cloud-Native Application: Full Stack Deployment
major_assignments.description: Design, deploy, and operate a complete cloud-native application on AWS using Terraform for infrastructure-as-code. The application must be deployed with CI/CD automation, secured following the principle of least privilege, monitored with the three pillars of observability, and cost-optimized with documented spending analysis.
objectives: Define all infrastructure in Terraform with remote state, modules, and environment separation
Implement CI/CD pipeline with automated testing, security scanning, and blue-green or canary deployment
Apply defense-in-depth security with IAM least privilege, encryption, and compliance automation
Configure complete observability with metrics, structured logging, distributed tracing, and alerting
deliverables: Terraform repository with modules for VPC, compute, database, monitoring, and security
CI/CD pipeline configuration (GitHub Actions) with build, test, security scan, and deploy stages
Security assessment documenting IAM policies, encryption configuration, and compliance controls
Cost analysis report with current spending breakdown and optimization recommendations
Operations runbook covering deployment procedures, incident response, and disaster recovery

criteria: Terraform defines all infrastructure with no manual console configuration
CI/CD pipeline automates build, test, and deployment with rollback capability
Multi-AZ architecture provides high availability with automated failover
Application functions correctly with proper data persistence and API integration
Architecture diagram accurately represents all components and their interactions
CloudWatch dashboard provides complete operational visibility
Cost analysis includes clear charts of spending by service and optimization impact
Runbook is formatted for quick reference during incidents
Compute resources are right-sized based on utilization data
Storage services match access patterns (correct database and storage class selection)
Caching strategy reduces unnecessary compute and database load
Cost optimization achieved at least 20% savings compared to naive deployment
Terraform code is modular, documented, and follows consistent naming conventions
Security assessment covers all five areas: identity, network, data, compute, compliance
Cost analysis provides actionable recommendations with estimated savings
Operations runbook covers deployment, scaling, backup, restore, and incident response
Complete cloud-native deployment with Terraform IaC
Automated CI/CD with security scanning and safe deployment strategy
Defense-in-depth security with least privilege and encryption
Full observability and documented cost optimization

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing cloud architecture concepts, service selection, and practical configuration skills

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on labs deploying and configuring cloud services

major_assignments.description: Three major projects building progressively more complete cloud-native applications

midterm_examination.description: Written exam on cloud services, containers, Kubernetes, and serverless architecture

final_project.description: Comprehensive cloud-native application demonstrating all course concepts with full operational readiness

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. Cloud lab environments are time-limited.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late. Cloud resources must be cleaned up by the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of cloud architectures is encouraged. All Terraform code, configurations, and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Never share AWS credentials or access keys. Do not commit secrets to Git repositories.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need an AWS Free Tier account, Docker Desktop, Terraform, kubectl, and AWS CLI installed. Lab credits are provided for AWS usage beyond the free tier.
